title: Mixed nut brittle
subtitle: Hard caramel brittle with almonds and peanuts
excerpt: Amber caramel cooked to hard-crack stage, poured over a bed of toasted almonds and peanuts, and spread thin before it sets. A pinch of bicarbonate of soda stirred in at the end creates a slightly aerated texture, making the brittle easier to bite through than a dense toffee. Shatters into jagged pieces for sharing.
sourceNotes: Nut brittle (caramel cooked to hard-crack stage with nuts) is documented in American and British confectionery manuals from the nineteenth century. Peanut brittle is particularly well-documented in American domestic cookbooks of the late nineteenth century, and almond brittle (croquant) appears in French and British confectionery manuals of the same period. The use of bicarbonate of soda to aerate the caramel is an American technique documented from the early twentieth century. The recipe here follows the established formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed bag between sheets of baking paper. Thaw at room temperature — it softens in humid conditions.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Use a larger saucepan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for two weeks in an airtight tin in a cool, dry place. Humidity is the enemy — do not refrigerate.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required. The caramel is cooked to hard-crack stage at 165°C. A sugar thermometer is essential — at this temperature the caramel burns to a bitter compound within 30 seconds of overshoot.

glossaryTerms.definition: Nut brittle is caramel cooked to hard-crack stage — around 165°C — then spread thinly over nuts and cooled to a rigid sheet. At hard-crack temperature the sugar is almost entirely dehydrated, which gives the brittle its glass-like hardness. Bicarbonate of soda is stirred in off the heat: it reacts with the acid in the caramel to release carbon dioxide, creating tiny bubbles that lighten the texture and make it easier to bite. Work fast — at 165°C the caramel continues to darken off the heat and will burn if left in the pan.

content.text: is fast work: the caramel goes from amber to burnt in under a minute. Lay the nuts on the tray, have the bicarbonate of soda measured, and the tray lined before touching the sugar. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Line a large baking tray with baking paper and scatter the nuts evenly across it. Combine the sugar and water in a heavy saucepan and bring to the boil over medium-high heat without stirring. Clip the sugar thermometer to the pan. Cook, without stirring, until the caramel reaches 165°C and turns a deep amber colour.

Remove immediately from the heat — it will continue to darken in the pan. Stir in the butter, vanilla, and salt; the mixture will spit. Stir in the bicarbonate of soda; it will foam. If hot caramel touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Pour immediately over the nuts and use the back of a spoon to spread it thin. Leave to set undisturbed for 30 minutes until completely hard. Break into pieces by hand or with the back of a spoon.
